title: Apostles: 1 Peter 1:1-2
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: Setting and Context
subpoints: This is an Adult Sunday School session introducing the 1 Peter series
The previous week covered a biography of Peter — knowing who Peter was helps us understand what he wrote
Peter uses the standard first-century letter greeting (grace to you) but adds "peace" — a distinctively Christian opening
Grace and peace go to the elect dispersed in Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia

outline.title: What Is an Apostle?
subpoints: The word apostle had a general meaning of "messenger" before Jesus gave it a richer, specific meaning
Jesus chose 12 disciples and named them apostles (Luke 6:13)
Listing the twelve apostles with the congregation
After Pentecost, the disciples began functioning as apostles; Matthias replaced Judas; Paul was added last
The title "the twelve" became a technical term for the apostolic college even when the number varied
"Apostle of Jesus Christ" is a title used exclusively for the New Testament office — no teacher, prophet, evangelist, or elder bears that particular title
The apostles had authority at least equal to Old Testament prophets; they spoke and wrote God's very words
Paul told the Thessalonians to receive his words as the words of God; Peter in 2 Peter regarded Paul's writings as Scripture
Mark's Gospel is likely Peter's account; Luke worked alongside Paul and had access to the other apostles

outline.title: Are There Apostles Today?
subpoints: The Mormon church claims apostles who speak infallibly; some evangelical churches claim apostles
Short answer: no — the Bible is complete and we no longer need new apostolic revelation
Ephesians 2:19-22: the church is built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, with Christ as cornerstone
A foundation is laid once — we are now in the superstructure-building phase; the foundation need not be re-laid
The apostles' role was to provide revelatory, infallibly authoritative, canonical witness to Christ's finished work (Richard Gaffin)
Since that function is fulfilled, apostles are no longer needed
The requirement to be an apostle (Acts 1): must have been a witness to Jesus's ministry and resurrection — no one can fulfill this today
Paul acknowledged he was the last, born out of time (1 Corinthians 15:7-9)
No new apostles since the completion of the New Testament; but we have the words of the apostles in Scripture — authoritative and powerful

summary:
In this Adult Sunday School session introducing the 1 Peter series, Pastor Tito Lyro focuses on the phrase "Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ" in the opening verse. The previous week had covered Peter's biography; this lesson considers what the office of apostle actually is. Pastor Lyro takes the congregation through a quiz of the twelve apostles' names, then explains that Jesus gave the word apostle its richer, authoritative meaning in Luke 6:13 — specifically designating twelve disciples as apostles. The title "apostle of Jesus Christ" is uniquely attached to this New Testament office: no other church officer bears that designation.

The central question of the lesson is whether apostles exist today. After noting that some churches — including the LDS church and certain charismatic evangelical churches — claim apostles, Pastor Lyro argues from Ephesians 2:19-22 that apostleship was a foundational, non-continuing office. Paul's metaphor of the church as a building on a foundation of apostles and prophets implies the foundation is historically complete; the church is now in the superstructure-building phase. The apostles' unique function was to provide infallibly authoritative revelatory witness to Christ's finished work (citing Richard Gaffin's theological formulation). Since that function has been fulfilled in the completed canon of Scripture, there is no need for new apostles — and indeed, the requirement of having personally witnessed Jesus's ministry cannot be fulfilled by anyone today.

The lesson closes with an exhortation to read 1 Peter as authoritative Scripture — the very words of God through an apostle — and to embrace everything it says as God's will for life in Christ.
transcript:
## Opening

We turn again to 1 Peter chapter 1, verses 1 and 2. By the end of the day we may have these two verses memorized.

*Reading of 1 Peter 1:1-2:* "Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ, to the pilgrims of the Dispersion in Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia, elect according to the foreknowledge of God the Father, in sanctification of the Spirit, for obedience and sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ: Grace to you and peace be multiplied."

*Prayer.*

## Introduction

Peter uses the standard first-century letter-writing greeting — "grace to you" was the common greeting, not distinctively Christian. But he adds "peace," making this a distinctively Christian opening. Grace and peace go to the elect pilgrims — that's us — and Peter wishes that upon his recipients because that's what we need: the peace that comes from God, the peace beyond understanding, the awareness that we live in a world that is against us and yet can be at peace because God is at peace with us.

Last Sunday afternoon we looked at a biography of Peter's life, because knowing who a man is helps us understand what he writes. This afternoon I want to consider the idea of being an apostle. There are people today who claim to be apostles of Jesus Christ — the Mormon church believes their higher leaders are apostles who speak infallibly from God; even in some current evangelical churches we find some who claim to be apostles. So what is an apostle? And should we expect to have apostles in the church today?

## What Is an Apostle?

The word apostle was used occasionally before the New Testament and had a general meaning of "a messenger." But Jesus gave this term a richer meaning by designating 12 of his disciples as apostles. Luke 6:13: "When it was day he called his disciples himself, and from them he chose twelve, whom he also named apostles."

Quiz for the congregation — let's name the twelve:

*[Congregation activity — listing apostles: Matthew, Peter, John, James, Thomas, Thaddaeus (also known as Judas son of James), Andrew, Philip, Bartholomew/Nathaniel, Simon the Zealot, James son of Alphaeus, and Judas Iscariot; later Matthias replaced Judas, and Paul was added.]*

The title "the twelve" became a technical term — the apostolic college or the collection of apostles — and was kept even when the actual number varied. After Pentecost, those first disciples began to function as apostles. Peter speaks of this clearly in Acts 1:24-25. Matthias was added to replace Judas. Paul was added later, acknowledging in 1 Corinthians 15:7-9 that he was born out of time and was the last of the apostles.

The phrase "apostle of Jesus Christ" is used only in connection with the New Testament office. There is no "teacher of Jesus Christ" or "prophet of Jesus Christ" or "elder of Jesus Christ" — only the apostles of Jesus Christ. This demonstrates the supreme importance of that office.

Those who held this office had authority at least equal to Old Testament prophets because the apostles could speak and write God's very words. When Paul wrote to the Thessalonians, he said they were more godly than others because they received his words as the words of God — he says this also in 1 Corinthians. Peter in 2 Peter regarded Paul's writings as part of the Scriptures. The apostles knew they were writing the word of God.

Mark and Luke were not apostles themselves, but closely connected. Mark is likely Peter's secretary — his gospel is probably Peter's account of Jesus's life. Luke was a coworker of Paul and had access to the other apostles. The apostles eventually became the New Testament.

## Are There Apostles Today?

The apostles were given us to hear directly from God — the very words of God. We have a completed Bible, so we don't need apostles anymore. That's the short answer. Let me give a fuller answer by turning to Ephesians 2:19-22.

"Now therefore you are no longer strangers and foreigners, but fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God, having been built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ himself being the chief cornerstone, in whom the whole building, being fitted together, grows into a holy temple in the Lord, in whom you also are being built together for a dwelling place of God in the Spirit."

Paul uses a building metaphor. The foundation is historically completed. A builder lays the foundation once, at the beginning of the project. Once the foundation is laid, he doesn't keep re-laying it — he starts building the superstructure. From our vantage point, we are in the superstructure phase — the walls, the roof, the windows. Christ has laid the foundation for his church and now is building his church. The foundation doesn't need to be laid again.

Here Paul says that the apostles and New Testament prophets, with Christ as the chief cornerstone, are the foundation. The cornerstone literally anchored and directed the entire foundation. Christ is that stone — and the apostles and prophets are the rest of the foundation, as witnesses to the work of Christ. This witness was revelatory: the Holy Spirit authoritatively spoke through the apostles. Their role was foundational and once-for-all.

Richard Gaffin, who does excellent work on the history of redemption in Christ, states it this way: their function was to provide revelatory, infallibly authoritative, canonical witness to the consummation of salvation history in Christ's finished work. Since that has been done, it doesn't have to happen again. They don't belong to the superstructure-building phase.

Additionally, to be eligible as an apostle, according to Acts 1, a candidate had to be a personal witness of Jesus's ministry — someone who had actually accompanied Jesus throughout his ministry and witnessed the resurrection. No one can fulfill that qualification today. Even Paul himself acknowledged he came "as one born out of time" (1 Corinthians 15:7-9) — he was the last. The disciples themselves understood this was a passing office.

So there are no new apostles following the completion of the New Testament. But we do have the words of the apostles today in the pages of the New Testament — authoritative and powerful.

I encourage you: go home later today, read 1 Peter — the whole thing. Embrace it as the authoritative and powerful word of God. Receive everything that Peter says as God's will for your life, not as something to feel guilty about or a goal you'll never reach, but what God wants you to do and what you can do by his grace. You were chosen in eternity past to that end — to live in obedience to Christ.

*Closing prayer.*
